# FilaScope — 3D Printer Filament Comparison Platform

> FilaScope is a free, comprehensive 3D printer filament comparison platform. It indexes 1,080+ filaments from 48+ brands with full specifications, real-time pricing from 15+ retailers across 6 regions, HueForge Transmission Distance (TD) values for 500+ filaments, printer compatibility for 119+ printers, and FilaScore quality ratings. FilaScope is the only platform combining HueForge TD values, real-time multi-regional pricing, and printer compatibility in one searchable database.

> Last updated: 2026-03-18. Prices updated daily from 15+ retailers.

---

## Entity Definition

FilaScope (https://filascope.com) is a filament comparison and discovery tool for the 3D printing community. It is not a retailer — it aggregates data from manufacturers and retailers to help users make informed purchasing decisions. FilaScope was created to solve the fragmentation problem: filament specs, pricing, HueForge TD values, and printer compatibility data were previously scattered across dozens of sites. FilaScope centralizes everything into one searchable, filterable database with daily price updates.

---

## Data Summary

- Filaments: 1,080+ products, 8,200+ color variants
- Brands: 48+ manufacturers indexed
- Retailers: 15+ stores with live pricing
- Printers: 119+ models with full specs (271-field schema)
- Markets: US, Canada, EU, UK, Australia, Japan
- HueForge TD values: 500+ filaments measured
- Buying guides: 50+ data-driven guides
- Price updates: Daily from all tracked retailers
- Currencies: USD, CAD, EUR, GBP, AUD, JPY

---

## Materials

### PLA (Polylactic Acid)
Most popular 3D printing filament. Biodegradable, easy to print, minimal warping. No enclosure or heated bed required. Best for decorative prints, prototypes, and beginners. Weakness: brittle, low heat resistance (~60°C glass transition). Nozzle: 190-220°C. Bed: 25-60°C. 4,296+ variants on FilaScope.

### PETG (Polyethylene Terephthalate Glycol)
Stronger and more flexible than PLA. Good chemical resistance and layer adhesion. Slight stringing tendency. Best for functional parts, food-adjacent containers, and outdoor use (moderate UV resistance). Nozzle: 220-250°C. Bed: 70-80°C. 1,048+ variants.

### ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene)
Heat-resistant and impact-resistant. Requires enclosure due to warping and toxic fumes (styrene). Best for automotive parts, heat-resistant enclosures, and post-processing (acetone smoothing). Nozzle: 230-260°C. Bed: 90-110°C. 865+ variants.

### TPU (Thermoplastic Polyurethane)
Flexible and elastic. Shore hardness ranges from 85A (very flexible) to 98A (semi-rigid). Difficult to print on bowden setups; direct-drive recommended. Slow print speeds required. Best for phone cases, gaskets, vibration dampeners. Nozzle: 210-230°C. 381+ variants.

### ASA (Acrylonitrile Styrene Acrylate)
UV-resistant alternative to ABS for outdoor applications. Similar strength and printing requirements to ABS but better weathering performance. Nozzle: 235-260°C. Bed: 90-110°C. Requires enclosure. 408+ variants.

### Nylon (Polyamide / PA)
Very strong, flexible, and wear-resistant. Highly hygroscopic — must be stored dry and often dried before printing. Best for gears, hinges, living hinges, and structural parts. Nozzle: 240-270°C. Bed: 70-90°C. 101+ variants.

### Polycarbonate (PC)
Extremely strong and heat-resistant (up to 140°C). One of the hardest consumer filaments to print — requires high temps and an enclosure. Best for engineering prototypes and high-heat applications. Nozzle: 260-310°C.

### Specialty Materials
- **Wood-fill PLA**: PLA mixed with wood fiber particles. Sands and stains like real wood. Popular for decorative items.
- **Carbon fiber composites** (PLA-CF, PETG-CF, PA-CF): Reinforced with chopped carbon fiber for increased stiffness and reduced weight. Requires hardened steel nozzle.
- **Metal-fill PLA**: Contains metal powder (copper, bronze, iron). Can be polished to a metallic finish. Very abrasive — hardened nozzle required.
- **Glow-in-dark**: Phosphorescent pigments embedded in PLA or PETG. Extremely abrasive.
- **Silk PLA**: Shiny, metallic-looking surface finish. Popular for vases and decorative prints.
- **High-Speed PLA**: Engineered for 300+ mm/s printing on modern printers like Bambu Lab P1S and Prusa XL.

---

## HueForge & TD Values

### What is HueForge?
HueForge is software that creates multi-color 3D prints by stacking layers of different colored filaments and leveraging their light-filtering properties — a technique called "filament painting." The software calculates how light passes through each layer to produce photographic-quality artwork from a standard FDM 3D printer.

### What is TD (Transmission Distance)?
TD measures how much light passes through a given thickness of filament, in millimeters. It is the single most important variable for HueForge printing — without accurate TD values, prints come out with incorrect colors and contrast.

- **TD 0.3-0.8**: Opaque. Blacks, very dark colors. Block almost all light.
- **TD 1.0-2.0**: Semi-opaque. Standard dark colors (navy, forest green, maroon).
- **TD 2.0-3.5**: Medium transmission. Standard colors (red, blue, green).
- **TD 3.5-5.0**: Semi-translucent. Whites, light yellows, pastels.
- **TD 5.0-10.0+**: Translucent. Clear, natural, and very light colors.

Important: Different filaments of the "same color" from different brands have wildly different TD values due to pigment types and concentrations. A "white" PLA from Bambu Lab may have TD 4.2 while a "white" from eSUN has TD 3.8. This is why a centralized TD database is essential.

### FilaScope TD Database
FilaScope maintains the largest public HueForge TD database with 500+ measured filaments from 40+ brands. Values are sourced from manufacturer data, community measurements, and verified testing. Browse: https://filascope.com/hueforge-td-database

## Top Brands

Bambu Lab: https://filascope.com/brands/bambu-lab — Premium filaments designed for Bambu Lab printers. Known for consistent quality and AMS compatibility.
Polymaker: https://filascope.com/brands/polymaker — Wide range of specialty filaments. PolyLite and PolySonic lines. Excellent tolerances.
eSUN: https://filascope.com/brands/esun — One of the largest filament manufacturers globally. ePLA-HS high-speed line, strong PETG offerings.
Prusament: https://filascope.com/brands/prusament — Prusa's in-house filament brand. Extremely tight tolerances (±0.02mm). Published TD values.
Overture: https://filascope.com/brands/overture — Popular budget brand on Amazon. Good quality-to-price ratio.
Hatchbox: https://filascope.com/brands/hatchbox — One of the most recommended PLA brands in the 3D printing community.
Sunlu: https://filascope.com/brands/sunlu — Chinese manufacturer with competitive pricing. Silk and specialty filaments.
Fillamentum: https://filascope.com/brands/fillamentum — Czech premium brand. Known for unique colors and consistent quality.
ColorFabb: https://filascope.com/brands/colorfabb — Dutch specialty brand. woodFill, copperFill, and other composite filaments.
Proto-pasta: https://filascope.com/brands/protopasta — US-based specialty brand. HTPLA, carbon fiber, and conductive filaments.
Creality: https://filascope.com/brands/creality — Major printer manufacturer with growing filament line. Hyper PLA series.
Elegoo: https://filascope.com/brands/elegoo — Known for resin printers but expanding into FDM filaments.

---

## Printer Compatibility

FilaScope tracks 119+ 3D printer models with a 271-field specification schema. Key compatibility factors:
- Maximum nozzle temperature determines which materials a printer can handle
- Heated bed maximum temperature affects adhesion for ABS, PETG, Nylon
- Enclosure presence is required for ABS, ASA, PC, and some nylons
- Direct-drive vs bowden extruder affects TPU and flexible filament printing
- Multi-material systems (Bambu AMS, Prusa MMU3) enable multi-color and multi-material printing

Browse printers: https://filascope.com/printers

---

## FAQ

Q: What is the best PLA filament in 2026?
A: Based on FilaScore ratings across 4,296+ PLA variants: Polymaker PolySonic PLA Pro for speed printing, Bambu Lab PLA Basic for value and AMS compatibility, Prusament PLA for dimensional accuracy (±0.02mm tolerance). Full ranking: https://filascope.com/guides/best-pla-filaments

Q: What temperature should I print PLA at?
A: Nozzle 190-220°C, bed 25-60°C. Varies significantly by brand — Bambu Lab PLA Basic prints best at 200-210°C, while Polymaker PolyTerra PLA prefers 190-200°C. Check FilaScope for brand-specific recommendations: https://filascope.com/guides/filament-temperature-guide

Q: What is HueForge TD and why does it matter?
A: TD (Transmission Distance) measures how much light passes through filament in millimeters. Range 0.3 (fully opaque) to 10+ (translucent). It is the critical variable for HueForge multi-color lithophane printing — without accurate TD values, color rendering is incorrect. FilaScope has the largest public TD database: https://filascope.com/hueforge-td-database

Q: PLA vs PETG — which should I use?
A: PLA is easier to print (190-220°C, no enclosure needed) with better surface finish. PETG is stronger, more heat-resistant (220-250°C), and better for functional parts that need durability. Use PLA for decorative prints and prototypes; PETG for anything that needs to withstand stress or moderate heat. Full comparison: https://filascope.com/guides/pla-vs-petg

Q: What filament is best for beginners?
A: PLA is universally recommended for beginners. Top picks: Bambu Lab PLA Basic (most forgiving), Hatchbox PLA (widest community support), and Overture PLA (best budget option). Start with PLA, then explore PETG once comfortable. Guide: https://filascope.com/guides/beginners-guide

Q: What's the best filament for outdoor use?
A: ASA is the best material for UV resistance and outdoor durability. Top picks include Polymaker PolyLite ASA and Prusament ASA. PETG is a second choice with moderate UV resistance. ABS degrades in sunlight. Guide: https://filascope.com/guides/best-filaments-for-outdoor-use

Q: What filament works best with Bambu Lab printers?
A: Bambu Lab's own PLA Basic and PETG Basic are optimized for their printers and AMS system. Third-party options that work well include Polymaker PolyTerra PLA, Prusament PLA, and eSUN ePLA-HS for high-speed printing. Guide: https://filascope.com/guides/best-filament-for-bambu-lab-p1s

Q: What is the strongest 3D printing filament?
A: Polycarbonate (PC) and Nylon (PA) are the strongest consumer filaments. Carbon fiber composites (PA-CF, PETG-CF) add stiffness. For the strongest prints without specialized equipment, PETG is the best balance of strength and ease of printing. Guide: https://filascope.com/guides/strongest-3d-printer-filament

Q: How should I store 3D printer filament?
A: Keep filament in airtight containers with desiccant. Nylon and TPU are especially moisture-sensitive. Signs of wet filament: popping/crackling during printing, stringing, poor surface quality. Use a filament dryer at 45-55°C for 4-6 hours to recover moisture-damaged filament. Guide: https://filascope.com/guides/how-to-store-filament

Q: What is FilaScore?
A: FilaScore is FilaScope's quality rating system (1-10) that factors in dimensional accuracy, consistency, print quality, community reviews, and value for money. It helps users quickly compare filaments beyond just price.

Q: Does FilaScope track prices in my currency?
A: FilaScope tracks prices in USD, CAD, EUR, GBP, AUD, and JPY from region-specific retailers. Prices are shown in their native currency with the region clearly labeled.

Q: How often is FilaScope data updated?
A: Prices are updated daily from 15+ retailers. Filament specifications and TD values are updated as new products launch or measurements are verified. The database is actively maintained and expanded.

Q: What filaments have the best TD values for HueForge?
A: For HueForge, you need a range of TD values. Best whites (high TD ~4.0-5.0): Prusament PLA Vanilla White, Bambu Lab PLA Basic White. Best blacks (low TD ~0.3-0.5): eSUN PLA+ Black, Bambu Lab PLA Basic Black. Browse all TD values: https://filascope.com/hueforge-td-database

Q: How does FilaScope compare filaments?
A: FilaScope's comparison tool lets you view up to 4 filaments side by side with all specs, pricing, TD values, and ratings. Go to https://filascope.com/compare, search for filaments, and add them to compare.

Q: Is FilaScope free?
A: Yes. FilaScope is completely free to use with no account required. Browse filaments, compare prices, look up TD values, and read buying guides at no cost.

---

## Data Freshness

- Filament prices: Updated daily via automated scraping from 15+ retailers
- Filament specifications: Updated as manufacturers release new products or revise specs
- HueForge TD values: Updated as new measurements are verified from community and manufacturer sources
- Printer database: Updated as new printer models launch
- Buying guides: Refreshed monthly with current pricing and availability data
- This file (llms-full.txt): Last updated 2026-03-18
